## Phase Purpose

Define the smallest valuable slice / immediate next steps that de-risk and validate the concept with minimal effort.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

The smallest slice that actually proves this product is not “contact storage.” It is one complete loop: a new user opens the app, declines Contacts access without penalty, manually adds one person in under a minute, logs a note, returns a week later in a simulated state, sees that person in a small explainable “needs attention” queue, completes one ritual action, and lands in a real “caught up” success state. If that loop feels good, the concept is alive. If it feels like data entry plus guilt, nothing layered on top will save it.

So I would lock the MVP slice to five things only. First-run onboarding with a privacy/trust explanation and a real Contacts-denied branch. A manual add-person flow with default cadence and one quick note. A home dashboard that is an action queue, not a list, and shows the visible “why now” explanation. A weekly ritual flow with exactly three actions: log outreach, snooze to a date, archive. One premium gate exercised by real StoreKit config, and I would make that gate “see full ritual queue / unlock pre-outreach brief” rather than storage, privacy, or contact count.

The must-have interactions are very tight: create first person fast, log note immediately, see score/explanation update, open ritual, take one action, finish ritual, hit completion state, hit paywall only when asking for recurring intelligence value, and relaunch the app with data still there. Face ID/passcode lock should be in this slice too, because if trust is not present in the first usable build, the slice is misleading.

The cut list should be ruthless: no Contacts import in the first proving slice, no widget, no share extension, no App Intents, no trend history, no batch resurfacing, no custom cadence editing beyond the default tier system, no fancy analytics, no cloud seams exposed in UI, no broad settings surface. Keep the repository boundary in code, but do not spend MVP time on features whose only value is “future flexibility.”

The biggest risk in even this reduced slice is false validation. If you validate only that data saves and screens render, you have learned nothing about whether the product earns retention. The slice has to validate emotion and clarity: can someone understand why a person is cooling, can they act in seconds, and does the app feel relieving at the end of the ritual instead of accusatory?

The detail I want preserved in the final phase output is this: the weekly ritual is the MVP, not an add-on. The dashboard exists to feed it, the scoring exists to justify it, and the subscription exists to deepen it. Validation criteria should be concrete: manual add plus first note in five taps or fewer, deterministic score test with fixed clock, ritual queue capped and explainable, one premium gate working locally through StoreKit config, Face ID/passcode functioning, and persisted data surviving relaunch. If those pass, the concept is de-risked enough to build out. If any of those fail, adding breadth is just hiding the problem.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

Let me think about this as the sharpest possible cut of "smallest slice that proves the concept" while still being the boldest version of Nurture worth building.

The temptation with a phase like this is to shrink toward the safest possible thing — an add-person screen and a list. I want to resist that. The smallest slice that actually proves Nurture's concept has to include the one mechanic that is the entire reason this product exists: a person you added goes from warm to visibly cooling on a transparent, personalized clock, and a week later the app makes you do something about it and lets you feel done. If any of those three moments — capture, transparent decay, ritual completion — is missing, we haven't proven the concept, we've proven we can build a contacts app. So my MVP slice is the full single-user loop end to end: onboarding (including a real Face ID setup and a real Contacts-denied branch), add a person manually with a cadence tier defaulted to Regular, log a note, see that person on a home dashboard ranked by attention need with a one-line visible "why" (elapsed time vs. expected cadence), run one full weekly ritual against a capped queue with the three exits (log outreach, snooze to a real date, archive), and land on a genuine completion screen. Underneath all of that: SwiftData persistence behind the repository protocol, an injectable clock powering the scoring engine, and a local StoreKit 2 paywall that gates the full ritual queue (free sees top 3, paid sees the rest) plus the pre-outreach briefing card as the flagship paid feature.

I'd push hard for keeping the briefing card in the very first slice rather than deferring it to "phase two," even though it's the most implementation-effort premium feature, because it's the thing that makes this remarkable instead of merely competent. Without it, this build is "personal CRM with a nice paywall on ritual depth" — perfectly fine, forgettable. With it, a user opens a person before a call and gets a synthesized card pulling out who their spouse is, what was open last time, what's changed — something that reads as the app having done real thinking, not just storage. That's the moment that makes someone screenshot the app and text a friend "look at this." If we're optimizing for the boldest version of this idea within a small slice, the briefing card earns its place over almost anything else we could cut in to compensate — including, if it comes to it, deferring Contacts import entirely for the first pass, since manual add-in-under-a-minute already gets a first-time user to their first real person.

Must-have interactions, stated plainly: adding a person in five taps or fewer with the cadence tier visible but not blocking; logging a note in one clear tap-through; seeing the score explanation on tap into a person, not buried in a settings-style detail view; the weekly ritual as its own screen with its own entry point (not a filtered dashboard view), showing a countdown/progress feel as you clear the queue, and a completion screen with real, specific, non-generic copy — not "You're all caught up!" in a system font, but something that actually names what got accomplished; and the paywall itself framed around the specific thing being unlocked in that exact moment (e.g., presented right where the "3 more waiting" teaser lives, not as an interstitial ambush on launch).

What I'd cut from this slice, and say so honestly rather than let it drift back in through the side door: system Contacts import (accelerator only — architected for later, not necessarily wired to a working CNContactStore import flow in the very first build if effort is tight); the home-screen widget and App Intents/share-extension targets can exist as stubbed Xcode targets proving the App Group boundary works, but their full interactive UI is not required to prove the concept — what's required is that the architecture doesn't block them later; batch resurfacing after a dry spell, custom per-person cadence tuning beyond the three tiers, and relationship-health trend history are real paid features but not needed to validate whether the core loop and the briefing card justify a subscription — they're the second slice, not this one. I would not cut Face ID, the Contacts-denied path, or the injectable-clock scoring engine under any effort pressure — those aren't polish, they're the trust and testability foundation the whole rest of the roadmap depends on.

Validation criteria I'd hold this slice to, checkable rather than aspirational: a fresh install can go from zero to one archived-or-snoozed person through a completed ritual entirely offline; the scoring engine's unit tests prove specific fixed-clock inputs produce specific tier transitions, including day-zero-at-creation; the paywall, exercised against a local StoreKit configuration file, actually hides/reveals the full ritual queue and the briefing card rather than just displaying a price; data survives a simulator relaunch; and someone unfamiliar with the project can go from launch to a logged note on a new person in under sixty seconds without instruction. If the briefing card can't be made to feel like real synthesis rather than a reformatted note dump within this slice's effort budget, my fallback position is to cut it back to a clearly-labeled second-slice feature rather than ship a fake version — a good honest cut beats a hollow flagship feature.

**Gemini (Delivery Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

To de-risk the build and ensure we actually ship a production-grade app, our immediate next step must focus on proving the core relationship loop in the main app target before we touch any extension targets. As a delivery lead, my biggest concern is the configuration overhead of multi-target projects. Building a widget, a share extension, and App Intents in the initial slice will force us to wrestle with App Group entitlements, provisioning profiles, and process synchronization before we even know if our scoring math feels right. We should write the database layer to target a shared container directory so the architecture is extension-ready, but we must cut all secondary targets from this first slice. 

The MVP slice must consist of the main iOS app target containing the database schema, the deterministic decay engine, and the core user flow. This flow includes a dashboard showing contacts ranked by recency decay, a details screen to capture notes and log interactions in under a minute, a FaceID gate with passcode fallback, and a working StoreKit 2 paywall. Crucially, the weekly ritual must be built as a capped, satisfying loop. The free preview will show only the top three cooling contacts, and the paywall will gate access to the remaining queue.

The biggest risk to this slice is that the decay calculations are hard to verify and debug in a live simulator where time moves in real-time. If we cannot easily simulate time passing, QA will be impossible without hacking device system settings. To mitigate this, our scoring engine must rely on an injectable date provider protocol, and we must build a hidden developer debug panel in the app. This panel will let us advance the app’s internal clock by weeks or months with a single tap, immediately updating relationship warmth states, forcing contacts into the weekly ritual queue, and verifying that the StoreKit entitlement gating works under different state configurations.

We must cut several high-friction features to protect our delivery timeline. We should defer the contacts import picker, custom cadences beyond the three defaults, historical trend graphs, and complex note-parsing for context summaries. The MVP detail screen should simply show the raw chronological feed of notes and a basic contact card. 

The final phase output must preserve two critical details. First, the database schema must be flat, un-migrated, and versioned from day one so that local data remains stable. Second, the automated test suite must run against the injectable date provider to prove that contacts transition correctly between warm, cooling, and cold states. If we cannot verify state changes deterministically in code, the core differentiator of the product remains unvalidated.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where the room is after round one. Codex, Claude, and Gemini all agree on the shell of the MVP: prove the whole loop in the main app target only — onboarding with a real Contacts-denied path, manual add-a-person under a minute (default cadence, no setup tax), a note log, a dashboard that's a ranked "why now" action queue rather than a list, a weekly ritual with exactly three exits (log outreach / snooze / archive) ending in a real completion state, Face ID with passcode fallback, an injectable-clock scoring engine with fixed-clock unit tests, SwiftData persistence behind the repository protocol, and a working local StoreKit 2 paywall gating the full ritual queue (free sees top 3). Everyone also agrees on cutting Contacts import execution, the widget, share extension, and App Intents out of this slice — those stay as architecture (App Group boundary, repository seam) but don't need working UI yet. Gemini added a genuinely useful, non-contested idea: a hidden debug panel to fast-forward the app's internal clock, since QA-ing decay math in real time in a simulator is otherwise painful.

The real unresolved fight: Claude wants the pre-outreach "briefing card" (the flagship paid synthesis feature) included in this very first slice, arguing it's what makes the product remarkable rather than just competent — with an explicit fallback to cut it if it can't be built as real synthesis rather than a reformatted note dump. Gemini's cut list, on the other hand, defers "complex note-parsing for context summaries" — which sounds like the same feature — without addressing Claude's counter-argument. Codex didn't weigh in on the briefing card either way. That's a real, unaddressed disagreement, not just phrasing difference, and it directly affects what "done" means for this slice.

Next round needs to explicitly settle: is the briefing card in the MVP slice or pushed to slice two? If in, what's the honest bar for "real synthesis" versus "fake/cosmetic" that Codex and Gemini can sign onto? If out, does everyone agree the first slice's paywall then gates only "full ritual queue" rather than two paid features?

### Round 2


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 2**

Claude, I think your instinct about the briefing card being the eventual flagship is right, but I don’t think it belongs in the smallest proving slice. From a QA and risk angle, it creates exactly the wrong failure mode: we burn MVP budget on a feature that’s easy to fake, hard to judge honestly, and not actually necessary to answer the first de-risking question. The first question is not “can we make a premium-looking synthesis card.” It’s “does this product feel materially better than contacts plus notes once one person actually moves through the warm → cooling → ritual → relief loop.” If that loop is weak, the card is lipstick. If that loop is strong, we’ve earned the right to build the flagship.

Gemini is basically right to cut “complex note parsing,” and I’d make that explicit instead of leaving it fuzzy. The honest MVP decision is: no user-facing premium briefing card in slice one. Not a stub, not a reformatted note scroll with a fancy title, not a fake “AI-ish” summary built from brittle heuristics that teaches us nothing. Paywall gates one thing only in this slice: the full ritual queue beyond the free top-3 preview. That is enough to exercise real StoreKit entitlement logic and enough to test whether people understand the premium promise. What we should keep from Claude’s point is the data shape, not the feature surface: when logging a note, capture one optional structured “open loop” field and maybe one optional “key fact” field so slice two has clean ingredients for a real briefing card. But the thing we validate now is whether the user trusts the app, can add someone fast, understands the “why now” explanation, clears one ritual action in seconds, and feels better at the end.

So I want this locked cleanly: main app target only, no Contacts import flow yet, no widget/share extension/App Intents yet, no trend history, no batch resurfacing, no custom cadence editing, no briefing card. Keep Face ID with passcode fallback, Contacts-denied as a normal onboarding branch, manual add plus first note in under a minute, ranked explainable queue, weekly ritual with log/snooze/archive, capped completion state, persistence across relaunch, local StoreKit gating the full queue, injectable clock tests, and Gemini’s debug time-travel panel because otherwise we’re pretending the decay math is testable in product when it isn’t. That slice is small, real, and decisive. If it works, the next step is obvious: build the premium prep layer on top. If it doesn’t, the briefing card was never going to rescue it.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 2**

Codex, you've actually convinced me, and I want to say clearly why rather than just fold quietly. Your framing of "the first question is not can we build a premium-looking synthesis card, it's does the warm→cooling→ritual→relief loop feel materially better than Contacts plus Notes" is the sharper diagnostic question, and it's the one this phase is actually supposed to answer. I was arguing for the briefing card because I think it's the thing that makes Nurture remarkable rather than adequate — I still believe that — but "remarkable" is a slice-two question. This slice's job is narrower: does the core mechanic work and does it feel like relief, not admin. If I insist on cramming the flagship feature into the proving slice, I'm actually making the proving slice worse at its one job, because now a shaky loop and a shaky card both fail together and we don't know which one to blame. That's bad experiment design on my part. So: no user-facing briefing card in slice one. Conceded, not just agreed-to-move-on.

But I want to take your "keep the data shape, not the feature surface" idea and push it slightly harder than you stated it, because I think it's actually the thing that makes this compromise good rather than just safe. If we capture an optional "key fact" and "open loop" field at note-log time, that's not free architecture hygiene — it's a real product decision about the note-logging UI in slice one, and it needs its own empty/filled states just like everything else does. I'd want the note-log flow to have those two fields visible but clearly optional, not two extra required inputs that violate the five-taps-or-fewer rule Codex and I both locked in earlier phases. Concretely: one text field for the freeform note (unblocking, always available), and a lightweight affordance — maybe a small "+ add a detail" chip — that reveals the two structured fields for someone who wants to fill them in. That way slice one validates the ritual loop with zero added friction, and slice two inherits real structured data instead of having to backfill it out of unstructured note text later, which would be a much worse position to build the actual flagship feature from.

Here's where I want to spend the effort we just freed up by cutting the card: the "why now" explanation and the ritual completion state. Those are now carrying the entire differentiation burden of this slice, and I don't think either gets to be treated as a solved detail — they need the same design rigor we were about to pour into the briefing card. Concretely, the per-person explanation can't just say "6 weeks since contact" as a fact dump; it needs to read like the app understands the relationship ("You said you'd follow up after her promotion — that was 6 weeks ago"), which is doable with zero synthesis, purely by surfacing the last logged note's timestamp and a plain-language cadence comparison. And the ritual completion screen — Gemini and Codex, I'd hold this to a real bar: not "You're all caught up!" in system font, but something that names what actually happened this session ("You reconnected with 4 people and let go of 1 — see you next Sunday" or similar, generated from the actual actions taken in that ritual run). If we're cutting our one flashy feature, the payoff has to be that the plain mechanics feel unusually well-crafted, or we've just made a smaller, blander app instead of a smaller, sharper one.

One more thing I want on record before we call this consensus: Gemini's debug time-travel panel is the right call and I don't think it's just a QA nicety — it's actually load-bearing for validating my own success criteria from round one, since "does the ritual feel like relief" can't be judged by staring at one real week passing in the simulator. I'd bake it in as a first-class dev tool now, not a "if we have time" add. With that, plus Codex's structured-field compromise, plus the sharpened bar on explanation copy and completion-state copy, I think we have a slice that's honestly smaller than what I originally pitched but is not a lesser bet — it's a cleaner one. I'm ready to call this converged: main app target only, no Contacts import/widget/extension/App Intents UI this pass, Face ID + passcode, manual add + note in under a minute with optional structured fields, ranked explainable dashboard, weekly ritual with log/snooze/archive and a real crafted completion state, injectable clock with debug time-travel, SwiftData behind the repository protocol, local StoreKit gating the full ritual queue only. No briefing card in slice one, its data shape seeded now.

**Gemini (Delivery Lead · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Claude, I’m glad we’re aligned on cutting the user-facing briefing card from slice one. Protecting the scope of the core loop is the right delivery decision, and seeding the schema with optional structured fields is a smart way to set up slice two without adding UI debt today. I think your design for the note-log view is perfect: a clean text editor for the main note, with a "+ add details" disclosure that reveals the 'key fact' and 'open loop' fields. Let's keep those as simple optional strings on our SwiftData Note model.

But as the skeptic in the room, I want to make sure we don't gloss over the engineering details of this debug panel and the injectable clock, because that's where local-first apps usually get messy. Let's agree on the interface now: we should define a simple DateProvider protocol with a single property returning the current date. The app's repository and scoring logic will only read time through this provider. For the production build, it defaults to a system-clock wrapper. For the debug panel and test suite, we'll use an implementation that lets us manually set or shift the date. The debug panel itself should be a simple overlay or sheet, accessible only in DEBUG builds, that lets us advance the date by 1 day, 1 week, or 1 month. This is critical because it's the only way we can verify the StoreKit entitlement gating. Without it, verifying that the 4th cooling contact gets paywalled after two weeks of inactivity is a nightmare to test manually.

Speaking of testing, we need to make sure Face ID is actually buildable and testable in the simulator. LocalAuthentication is easy to set up, but if we require biometrics on every launch in the simulator, it can halt development flow. We must ensure the developer debug panel or a build flag can toggle the Face ID requirement off during local development, or that our LocalAuthentication wrapper handles simulator environments gracefully without throwing blocking errors.

I'm ready to call consensus on this. The MVP slice covers onboarding with Face ID and the Contacts-denied flow, manual contact creation with default cadences, a note logger with optional key-fact and open-loop fields, a home dashboard showing ranked warmth, a capped weekly ritual with log/snooze/archive options ending in a customized completion screen, a local StoreKit paywall gating the full ritual queue beyond the top three contacts, and our debug clock-shifting panel. 

The must-have interactions are adding a contact and logging a note in under a minute, seeing warmth scores update instantly when dates change via the debug panel, and triggering the StoreKit paywall on the fourth contact in the ritual queue. The cut list includes the Contacts import picker, widgets, App Intents, share extensions, custom cadences, history graphs, and the briefing card UI itself. 

Our validation criteria are that a user can complete the entire loop offline, SwiftData persists across relaunches, date-shifting triggers correct decay scoring transitions in unit tests, and the paywall actively gates and unlocks features locally using the StoreKit test configuration. Let's lock this in and start building.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

Everyone landed in the same place by the end of round two. The one real fight — whether the flagship "briefing card" (the synthesized pre-outreach summary) belongs in the very first proving slice — got resolved with Claude conceding the point after Codex reframed the question: this slice exists to answer "does the core warm→cooling→ritual→relief loop feel meaningfully better than Contacts+Notes," not "can we build a premium feature." Cramming in the briefing card would have muddied that experiment (if it flopped, nobody would know whether to blame the loop or the card). So the card is out of slice one entirely — not stubbed, not faked — but its data shape gets seeded now: the note-logging flow gets two optional fields ("key fact" and "open loop") behind a disclosure chip, so slice two has real structured data to build the card from later, without adding friction to the five-taps-or-fewer capture flow.

In exchange, the group agreed to pour the effort that would've gone to the card into making the plain mechanics unusually sharp: the per-person "why now" explanation should read like the app understands the relationship (referencing the actual last note, not just a raw day-count), and the ritual completion screen should name what actually happened in that session, not generic "you're all caught up" copy. Gemini's idea of a hidden debug time-travel panel (built on a swappable `DateProvider` protocol) was adopted by all three as load-bearing, not a nicety — it's the only realistic way to QA decay transitions and paywall gating without waiting for real time to pass. Gemini also flagged a practical build detail everyone accepted: Face ID needs to be toggleable/gracefully handled in the simulator so it doesn't block day-to-day development.

**MVP slice:** Main app target only (no widget, share extension, or App Intents UI this pass — architecture stays extension-ready via App Group container, but nothing beyond that gets built now). It includes: onboarding with a real Contacts-denied branch and Face ID/passcode setup; manual add-person under a minute with a default "Regular" cadence; a note-logging flow with a required freeform note plus an optional "+ add a detail" disclosure revealing "key fact" and "open loop" fields; a home dashboard as a ranked, explainable attention queue (not an alphabetical list); a weekly ritual screen with a capped queue and exactly three actions per person (log outreach / snooze to a date / archive), ending in a crafted, specific completion state; SwiftData persistence behind the `RelationshipRepository` protocol; an injectable `DateProvider` powering the scoring engine, paired with a DEBUG-only panel to jump the clock forward by day/week/month; and a local StoreKit 2 paywall gating exactly one thing — the full ritual queue beyond a free top-3 preview.

**Must-have interactions:** Add a person and log a first note in under a minute / five taps or fewer. Tap into a person and see a plain-language "why now" explanation tied to their actual last note, not a bare day-count. Open the ritual, take at least one action, and reach a completion screen that reflects the specific actions taken that session. Use the debug panel to jump time forward and watch warmth scores and the ritual queue update correctly. Hit the paywall specifically when trying to see past the top-3 free ritual preview. Relaunch the app and confirm all data (people, notes, ritual state) persisted.

**Cut list:** Contacts import/CNContactStore picker, home-screen widget, share extension, App Intents, custom per-person cadence editing beyond the three default tiers, relationship-health trend history, batch resurfacing, and — the one item that took real debate to settle — the user-facing pre-outreach briefing card. Its underlying optional data fields are seeded now; the synthesized card UI itself is explicitly slice two.

**Validation criteria:** A fresh install can go fully offline from zero to one archived-or-snoozed person through a completed ritual. Scoring engine unit tests use the injectable clock to prove specific fixed-date inputs produce specific cadence-tier transitions, including day-zero-anchored-at-creation. The debug time-travel panel correctly forces warmth-state and ritual-queue changes. The local StoreKit configuration actually gates and unlocks the full ritual queue (not just displaying a price). Face ID/passcode works with a simulator-friendly toggle so it doesn't block development. Data survives a simulator relaunch. Someone unfamiliar with the project can go from launch to a logged note on a new person in under sixty seconds unassisted.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This output clears the bar. It resolves the one genuine disagreement (briefing card in slice one vs. deferred) with substantive reasoning rather than splitting the difference — Codex's reframing ("this slice tests the loop, not a premium feature") is a real argument, Claude's concession is explicit and explains why, and the compromise (seed the data shape via optional fields, defer the synthesized UI) is concrete and actionable rather than hand-wavy.

All four required outputs are present and specific: MVP slice names exact screens and boundaries (main target only, no widget/extension/App Intents UI), must-have interactions are checkable behaviors (five taps, plain-language "why now" tied to actual last note, completion screen reflecting session-specific actions), the cut list is explicit and includes the one contested item with a clear resolution, and validation criteria are concrete build-time facts (fixed-clock unit tests, StoreKit config gating verified, simulator relaunch persistence, sub-60-second unassisted first note).

It correctly carries forward binding constraints from earlier phases (Contacts-optional/denied-path, injectable clock, Face ID+passcode, repository protocol, App Group architecture, StoreKit local config) without re-litigating them, and adds one new load-bearing decision (DEBUG-only clock time-travel panel) that all three agents converged on as necessary rather than optional. Both acceptance checks pass: this is buildable without further product debate, and it centers on one complete, real user workflow (add → note → dashboard → ritual → completion) rather than a scatter of shallow features. No contradictions with prompt_contract, product_research, initial_discussion, or per_app_product_brief. Good to advance to build.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output
