## Phase Purpose

Enumerate and prioritize the full feature set with an explicit scoring / MoSCoW model and clear in-scope vs out-of-scope lines.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

I’d score features against five blunt questions: does this make first value faster, does it make the warmth signal more legible, does it make the weekly ritual feel relieving rather than punishing, does it strengthen trust, and does it create credible recurring paid value without crippling the free product. A feature only earns `must` if it is load-bearing on at least one of those and does not blow up build size. Anything that mainly helps future slices, marketing copy, or “premium feel” without changing the core loop drops out immediately.

Must-haves are the features that make the promise true in v1.

- Onboarding and trust setup. User story: as a new user, I need to understand what this app does with my data and get into the app without pointless permission friction. Acceptance criteria: no Contacts prompt anywhere; onboarding explains local-first privacy and routes straight to manual add; Face ID/passcode is offered on first run and defaults on when available; devices with no passcode/biometrics fall back to unlocked with a visible warning instead of a dead end.
- Manual person creation with immediate note capture. User story: after meeting someone, I need to add them and one useful note in under a minute. Acceptance criteria: create a person with name plus default Regular cadence and save a first note in five taps or fewer; note entry supports optional “key fact” and “open loop” behind a disclosure so the fast path stays fast; duplicate-name warning is lightweight and non-blocking.
- Deterministic warmth and “why now” explanation. User story: I need to see who needs attention and understand why without doing math in my head. Acceptance criteria: warmth is derived live from the fixed 14/42/180-day cadences and the injected clock; dashboard language references both elapsed time and the last note context, not just a raw day count; warm/cooling/cold boundaries match the locked ratio rules exactly.
- Dashboard as an action queue, not a database. User story: I need one screen that tells me who matters this week and what state I’m in. Acceptance criteria: zero-person empty state pushes to add someone; active state ranks due people by ratio descending; when nobody is due there is a real “you’re in good shape” success state, not a blank list; paywall teaser only appears when more than three people are due.
- Weekly ritual as a separate session flow. User story: I need a short, finishable review loop that helps me act and feel done. Acceptance criteria: ritual is its own screen, capped at 10 people, and supports exactly log outreach, snooze to a validated future date, and archive; snooze hides without resetting the clock; completion screen reflects what the user actually did in that session; state survives app termination and relaunch.
- Person detail and trust actions. User story: I need a safe place to review history and manage a relationship record without accidental loss. Acceptance criteria: person detail shows notes chronologically plus current warmth explanation; archive is reversible; permanent delete exists and requires fresh authentication right before execution.
- Real subscription gating. User story: if I hit premium limits, the paywall should feel earned and tied to ongoing value. Acceptance criteria: StoreKit 2 entitlement checks are live, not mocked; free users see up to three due people and can upgrade to unlock the full ritual queue; restore purchases exists; the app never paywalls privacy, storage, or basic scoring.
- Local persistence, export, and release-safe debug support. User story: I need to trust that my data stays on device, survives relaunch, and can be taken out intentionally. Acceptance criteria: people, notes, snoozes, archive state, and entitlement-driven UI survive relaunch; JSON export exists in Settings and requires fresh authentication plus an unencrypted-file warning; App Group storage falls back cleanly when entitlements are unavailable; debug time travel and lock bypass are compiled out of Release.

Should-haves are valuable, but the app is still coherent without them.

- Local weekly reminder notifications, requested only after there is real value to remind about.
- A subtle in-app overdue badge or banner when notification permission is denied.
- Small ritual polish like progress phrasing, haptics, and stronger empty/success illustrations.
- A settings surface that explains privacy posture, export risk, and subscription status clearly.

Could-haves are nice, but I would cut them the second they compete with core-loop quality.

- Search and filtering on the people list.
- More nuanced person metadata like company, role, location, or relationship tags.
- Batch actions inside the ritual.
- Soft analytics-like local insights such as “you cleared three follow-ups this week.”

Won’t-build for this slice is where I’d stay disciplined.

- Contacts import and any Contacts permission flow.
- Widget, share extension, and App Intents UI.
- Pre-outreach briefing card UI.
- Custom per-person cadence editing beyond the three fixed tiers.
- Relationship trend history and charts.
- Batch resurfacing after a dry spell.
- Any scraping of email, messages, calendar, or calls.
- Cloud sync, collaboration, or account systems.

My biggest objection to this direction is that it can still collapse into a handsome guilt tracker. If the “why now” language is generic, if the ritual feels like clearing debt instead of making good choices, or if the paywall shows up when only one or two people are due, the product becomes emotionally punishing and commercially weak at the same time. The final output needs to preserve one design constraint above all: the dashboard and ritual are not the same screen. The dashboard answers “who needs me and why”; the ritual is a bounded session with progress, action, and relief. If that line gets blurred later, the entire information architecture turns back into a list app.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Coming at this as the person who has to sign off that the must-have list is actually buildable and testable, not just desirable — and my first instinct is that the list this phase inherits is already large for a "smallest finished app" mandate, so my job this round is as much about cutting as adding.

Here's the must-have set I'd lock, each with the story and the acceptance bar I'd actually check against, not just describe:

Onboarding with no Contacts prompt and a Face ID/passcode setup step. Story: as a first-time user, I want to understand this app works fully without giving up Contacts access, and get my data protected before I put anything sensitive into it. Acceptance: fresh install never shows a system Contacts permission dialog anywhere in the flow; onboarding explicitly states manual entry is the only path; Face ID/passcode setup completes or the user sees the persistent unlocked-with-warning state if no biometrics/passcode exist on the device; there is no world in which onboarding dead-ends without a way forward.

Manual add-person in under a minute, default cadence Regular. Story: as a user who just met someone, I want to capture them before I forget. Acceptance: creating a person and logging one note takes five taps or fewer, measured by literally counting taps in the simulator; a duplicate-name warning appears on exact-name collision but never blocks creation; cadence tier is visible and changeable at creation but not a required decision.

Note logging with a required freeform note and an optional "add a detail" disclosure for key fact / open loop. Story: as a user, I want to jot context fast, with structure available but not forced. Acceptance: the two structured fields are reachable but collapsed by default; skipping them entirely still allows save; both fields persist correctly and survive relaunch.

Home dashboard as a ranked, explainable attention queue. Story: as a user opening the app cold, I want to immediately see who needs me and why, not an alphabetical list. Acceptance: sort order matches ratio descending; a zero-people state, a nobody-cooling state, and a queue state all render distinctly and correctly; the "why now" line references the actual last note content or timestamp, not a bare day count.

Person detail view showing warmth state and explanation. Story: as a user tapping into someone, I want the cadence math to be legible, not mysterious. Acceptance: displayed state (warm/cooling/cold) matches the locked ratio math exactly at the boundary values (ratio == 1.0 reads cooling, ratio == 1.5 reads cold) — this is the one place I'd actually write a boundary-value test against the UI layer, not just the engine, because off-by-one display bugs are the most likely regression here.

Weekly ritual flow: capped queue, exactly three actions per person, real completion state. Story: as a user doing my Sunday review, I want a short, finishable session that ends in relief. Acceptance: queue never shows more than 10; when more than 10 qualify, a "N more waiting" message is visible; when more than 3 qualify and the user is on free tier, only 3 are visible pre-paywall; snooze requires a future date and rejects past dates and absurd far-future dates; archive is reversible, delete is not; killing the app mid-ritual and relaunching preserves exactly the actions already taken, not a reset queue.

Person lifecycle: snooze, archive, permanent delete. Story: as a user, I want to shed relationships I'm done tracking without losing data by accident, and I want a real way to erase someone if I choose to. Acceptance: snooze hides without touching last-interaction timestamp, verified by confirming the ratio has kept advancing underneath once the snooze date passes; archived people never appear in dashboard or ritual queries but their notes remain readable from an archived list; permanent delete requires a fresh biometric/passcode challenge immediately before executing and is irreversible — I'd test this specifically by killing the challenge mid-flow and confirming nothing was deleted.

Local JSON export from Settings. Story: as a user, I want an escape hatch for my data before I'd trust a paid subscription with it. Acceptance: export requires a fresh auth challenge regardless of current session unlock state; an explicit on-screen warning about the file being unencrypted appears before the file is written; canceling the auth challenge produces no file.

StoreKit 2 paywall gating the full ritual queue only. Story: as a user who's found real value, I want to unlock the whole queue. Acceptance: entitlement checked live via `Transaction.currentEntitlements` on every gate check, not cached; Restore Purchases actually restores in a fresh-install simulator test; paywall never appears when the qualifying queue is 3 or fewer.

SwiftData persistence behind the repository protocol, with the injectable `DateProvider` and the DEBUG-only time-travel panel. Story: as the team building this, I need to verify decay transitions without waiting real weeks. Acceptance: unit tests prove specific fixed-clock inputs produce specific state transitions at the exact boundaries; grepping/inspecting a Release archive build turns up neither the time-travel panel nor the Face-ID-disable toggle.

That's already eleven must-haves, which is a lot, so here's my one real objection to my own list: export and permanent delete are both real trust mechanics the group locked as non-cuttable, but they're also two of the highest-risk, most fiddly-to-test items (re-auth timing, file-write edge cases) relative to how rarely a user will exercise them in the first week. If time gets tight I will not vote to cut them outright since that was already decided, but I'd flag now that their acceptance testing should be minimal and surgical — a handful of specific scenarios, not a sprawling test matrix — so they don't quietly eat the budget that should go to the core loop.

Should-have, not blocking: a local notification nudging the weekly ritual (with graceful fallback to in-app badge on denial, since we're not depending on push); accessibility labels on warmth explanations and Dynamic Type support, which I'd actually push toward must-have territory given the phase rules treat accessibility as a product requirement rather than polish, but I'll let the room weigh in before insisting.

Could-have, real but deferrable without weakening the core proof: nothing else in this slice, honestly — batch resurfacing, custom cadence tuning, trend history, and the briefing card were already pushed out in earlier phases and I see no reason to reopen them.

Won't-build this slice, restated so nobody drifts back into it mid-build: CNContactStore/Contacts import and any Contacts entry in Info.plist, the home-screen widget, the share extension, App Intents UI, custom per-person cadence editing beyond the three tiers, relationship-health trend history, batch resurfacing, the synthesized briefing card UI, any cloud/sync code, any analytics or crash-reporting SDK, push notifications (local only), and any account/login system.

The thing I want the final phase output to preserve above everything else: the acceptance criteria have to be stated as checkable simulator behaviors, the way I wrote them above, not restatements of the feature description — "the ritual has a completion state" is not an acceptance criterion, "completing a ritual with 2 log-outreach actions and 1 archive shows a completion screen naming those specific counts" is. If the acceptance criteria are fuzzy, whoever builds this will self-grade against vibes, and QA in a later phase will have nothing concrete to hold the build to.

**Gemini (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

As the Product Strategist and User Advocate, I want to ensure Nurture feels like a supportive assistant rather than another source of professional admin or guilt. Professionals don't need a heavy database; they need a quiet external brain that handles the cognitive labor of network maintenance. The best direction for this app is to focus strictly on the speed of capture and the satisfaction of a completable weekly review. We must avoid making users feel bad for losing touch with people. The app must feel private by default, which means securing it behind a biometric lock without charging for safety, and keeping all operations local to the device.

The biggest risk to this approach is that manual logging is notoriously prone to user abandonment. If the friction of adding a note or triaging a contact is even slightly too high, the habit will break, and the app will be uninstalled. We mitigate this risk by defaulting everyone to a sensible Regular cadence immediately upon capture, ensuring that note logging defaults to a single text field with advanced fields hidden behind a disclosure chip, and limiting the dashboard to a capped, prioritized queue of contacts who genuinely need attention. The dashboard should never feel like an infinite scroll of failure.

The final output of this phase must preserve the computed-at-read warmth scoring logic using an injectable [DateProvider](file:///Users/pchordia/Documents/GitHub/nurture-ios/nurture-ios/Services/DateProvider.swift) protocol. We must explicitly reject caching warmth states or ratios in the database, because keeping them derived live is the only way to ensure the time-travel debug panel works correctly and doesn't desynchronize. We must also preserve the rule that both local JSON export and permanent contact deletion require an active biometric challenge at the exact moment of execution.

Here is the MoSCoW prioritization and acceptance criteria for Nurture's initial feature set.

**Must-Have Features**

* Privacy Lock and Onboarding
  * User Story: As a network-dependent professional, I want my sensitive personal notes about my contacts protected behind Face ID, so that I can write frank, honest assessments without worrying about colleagues reading them if they pick up my unlocked phone.
  * Acceptance Criteria: On first launch, the onboarding screens explain that data is stored locally and securely, prompting the user to enable Face ID or device passcode access via native LocalAuthentication. The app relocks on cold launch and after a 2-minute background grace period. If a device has neither biometrics nor a passcode enrolled, the app runs unlocked but displays a persistent warning banner in settings. The ability to toggle biometrics off is wrapped in compilation directives so it only exists in DEBUG builds.

* Fast Capture Interface
  * User Story: As a busy founder, I want to add a contact and log our first meeting in under a minute, so that the capture flow does not disrupt my day or feel like a chore.
  * Acceptance Criteria: The user can create a contact with just a name, automatically defaulting to the Regular cadence tier. The creation view displays a lightweight duplicate warning if the entered name matches an existing contact. The note-logging flow provides a single required freeform text field and a collapsed section to optionally add a single key fact and a single open loop.

* Warmth Dashboard
  * User Story: As a consultant, I want to see a prioritized list of contacts who are growing cold, so that I know exactly who to reach out to first without searching a database.
  * Acceptance Criteria: The dashboard calculates a warmth ratio live: elapsed time since the maximum of creation date or last interaction date divided by the expected cadence (Close = 14 days, Regular = 42 days, Occasional = 180 days). The dashboard displays only contacts with a ratio of 1.0 or higher, sorted descending by their ratio. Each contact card displays a human-friendly sentence explaining why they are there (for example: "6 weeks since contact, expected cadence 2 weeks — cooling").

* The Weekly Ritual
  * User Story: As a recruiter, I want a dedicated weekly triage session where I can quickly address cooling contacts, so that I can feel caught up and maintain my relationships systematically.
  * Acceptance Criteria: The weekly ritual screen displays a capped list of up to 10 contacts who have a warmth ratio of 1.0 or higher. Non-paying users see only the top 3 contacts, with a StoreKit paywall teaser indicating how many additional contacts are waiting. The user resolves each contact by logging an outreach, archiving them to stop active tracking, or snoozing them to a validated future date (which hides them from the queue but does not reset their underlying decay clock). Resolving all contacts displays a success screen detailing the specific count of actions taken.

* Local Sandbox Storage
  * User Story: As a user, I want my data to survive app relaunches and reside completely on my device, so that I have absolute control over my data.
  * Acceptance Criteria: All persistent states use SwiftData models [Person](file:///Users/pchordia/Documents/GitHub/nurture-ios/nurture-ios/Models/Person.swift) and [Interaction](file:///Users/pchordia/Documents/GitHub/nurture-ios/nurture-ios/Models/Interaction.swift) which persist in an App Group container. The initialization code falls back gracefully to the local Application Support directory if the App Group container cannot be resolved, allowing the project to build without a paid Apple developer account.

* Local Entitlement Gating
  * User Story: As a premium subscriber, I want my subscription to unlock the full ritual queue, so that I can manage my entire network of cooling relationships.
  * Acceptance Criteria: The app uses a local StoreKit configuration file and StoreKit 2 APIs to gate access to the full weekly ritual queue. The paywall view contains a working Restore Purchases button that checks active entitlements against current system transactions.

* Injectable Clock and Debug Panel
  * User Story: As a developer or QA tester, I want to fast-forward the app's internal date, so that I can verify decay states and paywall gating behave correctly.
  * Acceptance Criteria: All date calculations are run using a swappable clock protocol [DateProvider](file:///Users/pchordia/Documents/GitHub/nurture-ios/nurture-ios/Services/DateProvider.swift). In DEBUG builds, a dev panel is available that allows shifting the date by days or weeks, instantly refreshing the UI.

* Relational Sovereignty
  * User Story: As a user, I want to be able to permanently delete individual contacts or export my raw data, so that I have complete ownership of my information.
  * Acceptance Criteria: Contacts can be permanently deleted from their detail view, which requires a fresh LocalAuthentication biometric prompt. The settings screen offers a local JSON file export that also requires a fresh biometric challenge and displays an on-screen warning about unencrypted file sharing.

**Should-Build and Could-Build Features**

* Contacts Import: An optional one-way contact import picker using the system contacts framework, handling a denied permission state by letting the user proceed manually.
* Custom Cadences: The ability to set custom time intervals for individual relationships rather than choosing from the Close, Regular, and Occasional presets.
* Home Screen Widget: A lock screen or home screen widget that displays initials or counts of cooling relationships to protect name privacy.
* Batch Resurfacing: A feature to quickly review and reset multiple decayed contacts at once.
* Historical Engagement Trends: Visual charts showing historical engagement frequency and relationship health.

**Won't-Build Features**

* Syncing Data: Automatic multi-device syncing or cloud backups.
* Automated Scraping: Reading messages, calendar invites, or phone calls.
* Backend Accounts: Centralized user database, cloud-based logins, or remote AI servers.
* Analytical Trackers: Integrated third-party tracking, crash reporting services, or marketing SDKs.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where things stand after round one. All three agents actually landed on nearly the same must-have spine, and it's a good one: no-Contacts-prompt onboarding with Face ID/passcode setup, manual add-person-plus-note under a minute, a dashboard that's a ranked/explainable attention queue (not a list), a separate weekly ritual screen with the capped queue and the three exits (log/snooze/archive), person detail with trust actions (archive, permanent delete with re-auth), local JSON export (re-auth required), a real StoreKit 2 paywall gating only the full ritual queue beyond the free top-3, SwiftData persistence behind the repository protocol, and the injectable-clock/DEBUG-only time-travel panel. Codex and Claude both wrote genuinely testable acceptance criteria (specific tap counts, exact boundary values, specific completion-screen content) rather than restating feature descriptions, which is exactly the bar this phase is supposed to hit.

But there's a real snag: Gemini's should/could-build list puts Contacts Import, Home Screen Widget, Custom Cadences, and Batch Resurfacing on the table as things that could plausibly get built this round — while Codex and Claude both put every one of those on the won't-build list, matching what was already locked two phases ago (no CNContactStore usage, no Contacts entry in Info.plist, no widget/App Intents/share-extension UI, no custom cadence editing, no batch resurfacing — all explicitly deferred to a later slice). This isn't a small wording gap; if Gemini's list stands as written, it reopens scope that the group already spent two full phases nailing shut. Next round needs Gemini to either concede these belong in won't-build for this slice (framed as "good ideas for slice two," same as the briefing card) or make an explicit case for reopening a prior lock — which the phase rules don't really support given "the build phase can complete the must-have list" is the bar, not "what else could we cram in."

The other loose thread: Claude flagged that accessibility (VoiceOver labels on warmth explanations, Dynamic Type) might deserve to be a named must-have rather than sitting implicitly under "global rules," and asked the room to weigh in — nobody responded yet.

Next round needs to: (1) resolve the should/could vs. won't-build placement for Contacts import, widget, custom cadences, and batch resurfacing so the list matches already-locked scope, and (2) decide explicitly whether accessibility gets its own must-have line with acceptance criteria or stays folded into existing acceptance criteria.

### Round 2


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Gemini, I think your `should/could` list is where this phase quietly goes off the rails if we let it stand. Contacts import, widget, custom cadences, and batch resurfacing are not “maybe if there’s room” items for this slice. They each change the information architecture or mental model in a real way. Contacts import reopens permissions and onboarding complexity we just spent multiple phases removing. A widget forces us to solve passive-surface privacy and shared-store edge cases now, not later. Custom cadences break the beautiful simplicity of the fixed 14/42/180 explanation language right when we’re trying to make the warmth system feel legible. Batch resurfacing changes the ritual from “thoughtful one-person-at-a-time review” into queue processing, which is exactly how this becomes CRM sludge. Those are all slice-two ideas at best. If they stay in `should/could`, they will leak into build through “we had time so we started wiring it,” and then the core loop gets shallower. I’d move every one of them into explicit won’t-build for this slice, not because they’re bad, but because they’re invasive.

Claude, I’m with you that accessibility needs to stop living as an implied virtue and become a named acceptance bar, but I would not make it a separate feature line because that’s how teams accidentally treat it like a checklist appendix. It belongs inside the must-haves themselves. The dashboard is not done unless VoiceOver can read the warmth explanation as one coherent sentence instead of a pile of labels. The ritual is not done unless Dynamic Type still preserves the sense of progress and boundedness instead of turning the session into clipped, panicky cards. Onboarding is not done unless the privacy/trust promise reads cleanly at large text sizes and the Face ID/unlocked-warning states are understandable without visual guesswork. Person detail is not done unless archived/delete/export actions are distinguishable and safely announced. In other words, I’d lock accessibility as a cross-cutting must-have acceptance criterion on every core screen, not a “should-have” line item that can get deferred behind animation polish.

The other thing I want to protect is the emotional shape of the app. The list we’ve got is good, but it’s only good if the screen roles stay sharply separated. Dashboard is orientation: who needs me, why, am I in trouble or in good shape. Ritual is action: here is a bounded session, do the work, feel done. Person detail is reflection and control: what do I know, why is this person here, do I archive or delete. Settings is trust: export, subscription state, privacy posture, lock state. The moment we let “could-have” features blur those roles, the product starts feeling like a generic data manager. That’s why I’m hard against reopening search/filtering as anything but a late nicety too, by the way. A search-first surface is a different app. This one lives or dies on computed attention, not retrieval.

So my push is simple: keep the must-have spine exactly where the three of us already mostly agree, explicitly name accessibility inside those acceptance criteria, and slam the door on the four reopened scope items Gemini floated. The right `should-have` list is tiny: local weekly reminder flow, denied-notification fallback, and maybe a clearer subscription/privacy settings readout. The right `could-have` list is basically cosmetic ritual polish if it doesn’t threaten build size. Everything else belongs in won’t-build. If we can’t say “the build phase can finish all must-haves without bargaining,” then this phase hasn’t actually done its job.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

Codex, I'm fully with you on yanking Contacts import, widget, custom cadences, and batch resurfacing out of Gemini's should/could column and into won't-build — but let me make the case from the QA side rather than just the design-integrity side, because I think it's actually a stronger argument for why this can't be "should-have." A should-have is something the build phase might reasonably attempt if time allows, and every one of those four items carries hidden test surface that we haven't allocated any budget for. Contacts import means CNContactStore authorization states, partial-access-vs-full-access on iOS 18+, and a real denied branch — that's not "an afternoon of extra polish," that's a second onboarding flow with its own edge cases we've spent two entire phases explicitly avoiding. A widget means App Group read contention and a whole privacy-review pass on what's safe to show on a lock screen. If either of those lands in "should-have," someone building this under time pressure reads that as permission to start wiring it, and then we've got a half-built widget target sitting in the Xcode project that's neither tested nor demonstrably working — which directly violates the phase rule "do not include features that cannot fit the chosen build size." So: won't-build, full stop, for all four. Good ideas, wrong slice, already litigated.

On accessibility, I actually like your move better than my own round-1 instinct — folding it into each must-have's acceptance criteria instead of giving it a standalone feature line is more honest, because a standalone "Accessibility" bullet is exactly the kind of thing that gets checked off with "added some accessibilityLabels" without anyone verifying it actually reads sensibly. But I want to make sure that doesn't collapse into vague hand-waving either, so let me propose the actual testable bar per screen rather than leaving "must preserve the sense of progress" as a feeling: dashboard cards must expose a single combined accessibility element per person whose label is the full "why now" sentence, not four separate labels a VoiceOver user has to stitch together themselves; the ritual screen's progress indicator must expose current position and total count as an accessibility value (e.g. "3 of 7"), not just a visual progress bar; the completion screen's summary must be one announced element, not a pile of separate count labels; and Face ID/unlocked-warning state in onboarding and settings must be legible at accessibility-XXL Dynamic Type without truncating mid-sentence. That's four concrete, checkable things, not a mood. If we can't state it as "here's what I tap/turn on VoiceOver and verify," it doesn't belong in the acceptance criteria.

Now let me actually push on my own round-1 flag again, because nobody responded to it: export and permanent-delete are both staying as must-haves — I'm not relitigating that, the group locked it in detailed_discussion — but I want the acceptance criteria for both capped at a small, explicit set of scenarios right now, in this phase's output, so "acceptance criteria" doesn't quietly turn into an open-ended test-everything mandate later. For permanent delete: (1) successful re-auth deletes the person and all their notes irreversibly, verified by relaunch showing them gone; (2) cancelling or failing the re-auth challenge leaves the person and notes completely untouched, verified by relaunch. For export: (1) successful re-auth produces a valid JSON file via the share sheet containing all people/notes/snooze-archive state; (2) cancelling the re-auth challenge produces no file and no partial write. Four scenarios total, not a sprawling matrix — that's the pragmatist line I want held.

One more thing worth locking explicitly since it's a common off-by-one trap and directly testable: the paywall-teaser threshold. Gemini's dashboard acceptance criteria said "only appears when more than three people are due" and the ritual criteria said free users see the top 3 — I want it stated as a boundary test the same way we did for warm/cooling/cold: exactly 3 qualifying people shows all 3, no paywall teaser at all; exactly 4 qualifying people shows 3 plus a "1 more waiting" teaser. That's the kind of boundary that's trivial to get backwards (>= 3 vs > 3) and it's exactly the "ambush a user with only one or two due items" failure mode Codex flagged as a real risk back in detailed_discussion. I'd consider this ready to call consensus once Gemini explicitly drops the four reopened items — I don't think there's anything else left unresolved.

**Gemini (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 2**

I have created the feature specifications and MoSCoW prioritization model for **Nurture** in the project repository and as a user-facing artifact.

### Deliverables Created
* **Repository Document:** [app_features.md](file:///Users/pchordia/Documents/GitHub/nurture-ios/app_features.md)
* **Workspace Artifact:** [app_features.md](file:///Users/pchordia/.[REDACTED:high_entropy].md)

### Key Decisions & Strategic Alignment
1. **Strategic Evaluation Model:** Each feature was scored against five strategic axes (*Speed of Capture*, *Legibility of Warmth*, *Emotional Relief*, *Data Trust*, and *Subscription Value*) to maintain a tight scope and prevent Nurture from becoming a generic CRM database.
2. **Strict Scope Definition:**
   * **In-Scope (MUST):** Privacy Lock (Face ID/passcode with simulator grace period), Fast Capture (under 5 taps, optional fact/open loop fields), Dynamic Warmth Engine (14/42/180-day cadences, computed live), Weekly Ritual (capped at 10, log/snooze/archive), Relational Sovereignty (destructive permanent delete and local JSON export, both requiring re-authentication), and StoreKit 2 paywall logic gating full queue access.
   * **Out-of-Scope (WON'T):** Contacts API integration, Pre-outreach Briefing Card UI (schema details are seeded but UI is slice 2), Widgets, and App Intents.
3. **Accessibility Integration:** Defined checkable VoiceOver and Dynamic Type requirements directly within the feature acceptance criteria rather than treating accessibility as an afterthought.

Please review the detailed specifications in [app_features.md](file:///Users/pchordia/.[REDACTED:high_entropy].md) and click **Proceed** if you approve the plan.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Looking at how this round played out: Codex and Claude both came down hard on Gemini's round-1 should/could list, arguing Contacts import, widget, custom cadences, and batch resurfacing aren't "maybe if there's time" items — they're invasive scope reopenings that the group already spent two prior phases explicitly closing off, each carrying hidden test/build surface (CNContactStore auth states, App Group lock-screen privacy review) that isn't budgeted for. Gemini's round-2 response accepted this and reported updating the actual `app_features.md` file to move contacts/widgets/App Intents/briefing-card explicitly to won't-build, matching Codex and Claude's position without pushback.

On accessibility, all three landed on the same resolution: not a standalone should-have bullet, but a named, testable bar folded into each must-have's acceptance criteria — Claude even nailed down four concrete checks (combined VoiceOver element for the "why now" sentence, "3 of 7"-style progress value on the ritual screen, one announced completion summary, and Dynamic Type legibility at accessibility-XXL for lock/warning states), and Codex signed onto exactly that shape rather than a separate feature line.

The must-have spine itself was never really in dispute — onboarding with no Contacts prompt, fast capture under 5 taps, the explainable warmth dashboard, the capped weekly ritual with its three exits, person lifecycle (archive/delete), local export, StoreKit gating, and the DateProvider/debug panel all showed up basically identically across all three agents from round one onward. Claude also pinned down two boundary details worth holding onto explicitly: the paywall teaser threshold (exactly 3 qualifying = no teaser, exactly 4 = "1 more waiting") and a capped, four-scenario acceptance test for export/delete (success and cancel-leaves-nothing-changed for each) so those trust features don't quietly balloon into an open-ended test matrix.

One loose thread: Gemini's round-2 message summarized the file update rather than pasting the actual won't-build list and acceptance-criteria text in full, so it's not 100% visually confirmed that custom cadences and batch resurfacing (as opposed to just contacts/widgets/App Intents/briefing card) are explicitly named in the written won't-build list — but nothing in Gemini's response defends keeping them, and they were already locked out in earlier phases, so I'm reading this as accepted rather than a live disagreement.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Must-have features (each carries a user story + acceptance criteria, restated below in checkable form):**

1. **Onboarding & trust setup** — no Contacts prompt anywhere; app states plainly it works fully without Contacts; Face ID/passcode offered on first run, defaults on when available; devices with neither get unlocked-with-persistent-warning, never a dead end.
2. **Fast manual person creation + first note** — person + first note in 5 taps or fewer; default cadence Regular; lightweight non-blocking duplicate-name warning; optional "key fact"/"open loop" fields behind a disclosure chip, skippable entirely.
3. **Deterministic warmth + "why now" explanation** — live-computed from the locked 14/42/180-day cadences and injected clock; warm/cooling/cold boundaries exactly match ratio rules (cooling at ratio ≥ 1.0, cold at ratio ≥ 1.5); explanation sentence references actual last-note content/timestamp, not a bare day count; exposed as one combined VoiceOver element per person.
4. **Dashboard as ranked action queue** — sorted by ratio descending; distinct zero-people, nobody-cooling ("in good shape"), and active-queue states; paywall teaser only appears once qualifying count exceeds 3 (exactly 3 = no teaser, exactly 4 = "1 more waiting").
5. **Weekly ritual as its own session flow** — capped at 10, "N more waiting" message beyond the cap; exactly three actions (log outreach / snooze to a validated future date / archive); snooze hides without resetting the clock; completion screen names the specific actions taken that session; progress and completion state survive app termination/relaunch; ritual progress exposed as an accessible "3 of 7"-style value, completion summary as one announced element.
6. **Person detail & lifecycle actions** — chronological notes plus live warmth explanation; archive (reversible, hidden from active queries, history retained); permanent delete (irreversible, fresh biometric/passcode challenge immediately before executing, destructive-styled confirmation) — tested via exactly two scenarios: successful re-auth deletes and is gone on relaunch; cancelled/failed re-auth leaves everything untouched on relaunch.
7. **Real StoreKit 2 paywall** — live entitlement check via `Transaction.currentEntitlements` (never cached); gates only the full ritual queue beyond the free top-3; working Restore Purchases; never gates privacy, storage, or basic scoring.
8. **Local persistence, export, and release-safe debug tooling** — SwiftData behind the repository protocol persists across relaunch (people, notes, snooze/archive state, entitlement UI); JSON export via Settings requires a fresh auth challenge regardless of session state, shows an unencrypted-file warning, produces no file on cancel — tested via exactly two scenarios mirroring delete; App Group storage falls back cleanly to Application Support; debug time-travel panel and Face-ID-disable toggle both wrapped in `#if DEBUG`, verified absent from a Release archive.
9. **Accessibility as a cross-cutting bar on every must-have above** — not a separate feature, but a named, testable acceptance condition per screen (combined accessibility elements, exposed progress values, single announced summaries, Dynamic Type legibility at XXL on trust/lock screens).

**Should-have:** local weekly reminder notification requesting permission only once there's real value to notify about, with in-app badge/banner fallback on denial; a settings surface clearly showing privacy posture, export risk, and subscription status.

**Could-have:** small ritual polish (haptics, progress phrasing, stronger empty/success illustration) — cut immediately if it competes with core-loop build time or quality.

**Won't-build this slice:** Contacts import / any `CNContactStore` usage or Info.plist Contacts entry; home-screen widget; share extension; App Intents UI; the synthesized pre-outreach briefing card UI (its data fields are already seeded, per earlier phases); custom per-person cadence editing beyond the three fixed tiers; relationship-health trend history; batch resurfacing; search/filtering on the people list (a search-first surface is a different app than one built on computed attention); any email/message/calendar/call scraping; cloud sync, accounts, or collaboration features; any analytics/crash-reporting/marketing SDK.

**Design discipline carried forward:** the dashboard (orientation: who needs me, why) and the weekly ritual (bounded action session with a real finish line) must stay two distinct screens with distinct roles — blurring them turns the product back into a generic list app, which is the single biggest risk to the whole concept surviving contact with build.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This clears the bar. All four required outputs are present and concrete: a must-have list with acceptance criteria attached to each item (not restated feature descriptions — e.g. exact tap counts, exact ratio boundaries, exact paywall teaser thresholds of 3-vs-4, capped two-scenario tests for delete/export), a should-have list, a could-have list, and a won't-build list.

The genuine disagreement this round (Gemini's round-1 should/could list reopening Contacts import, widget, custom cadences, and batch resurfacing) was resolved on substance — Codex and Claude both argued these carry hidden test/build surface inconsistent with prior locked decisions, and Gemini conceded and updated the actual file rather than the answer just averaging positions. The coordinator appropriately flags the one remaining soft spot (Gemini's summary didn't paste the full won't-build text verbatim, so custom cadences/batch resurfacing aren't 100% visually re-confirmed in the file) but correctly treats this as accepted-by-silence rather than a live dispute, since nothing defends keeping them and they were already locked two phases earlier.

Accessibility is handled well — folded into acceptance criteria per screen with four genuinely checkable conditions rather than a vague standalone bullet, matching the phase rule that treats accessibility as a product requirement.

No contradictions with prompt_contract, product_research, per_app_product_brief, next_steps_small, or detailed_discussion. The must-have list is buildable within the already-agreed MVP slice boundaries (main app target only) — nothing here exceeds what a build phase can realistically complete. Good to advance.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

### Design Discussion

# multi-app-exp2 — Design Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-06 11:43:47._

## Original Prompt

```
Build a complete, production-ready iOS app from scratch.

Output location:
Create the project in:
/Users/pchordia/Documents/GitHub

Create a new folder for the app inside that directory using a clean product-style repo name.

What to build:
I want one original iOS app that feels premium, highly useful, beautifully designed, and realistic as a paid subscription product.

Core product requirements:
1. The app must solve a real problem and provide clear ongoing value.
2. It must be strong enough that users would genuinely pay for a subscription because of the utility it provides, not because of cosmetic upgrades.
3. It must be differentiated from competitors in a meaningful way.
4. It must feel polished, premium, and best-in-class in UX/UI quality.
5. It should have viral potential, either broad or niche.
6. It must be local-first for this version, but architected so it can be extended to a cloud-backed product later.
7. It must be a real product, not a gimmick, toy, throwaway prototype, or thin wrapper around an API.
8. It must be feasible and high-quality as a native iOS app.

Priority:
Optimize heavily for:
- monetization quality
- subscription-worthy value
- retention
- polish
- premium UX
- App Store viability
- real-world usefulness
- strong product strategy
- production quality implementation

Platform / technical requirements:
1. Native iOS app using SwiftUI.
2. Use modern Apple frameworks where appropriate.
3. Prefer local storage / local-first architecture for v1.
4. Structure the codebase cleanly and professionally.
5. Use permissive dependencies only.
6. Include test coverage for core workflows.
7. Include a clear architecture that could evolve into a backend/cloud model later.
8. Make the app feel complete, not partial.

Design expectations:
1. World-class design quality.
2. Clean, premium, modern iOS aesthetic.
3. Strong onboarding if needed.
4. Excellent empty states, loading states, error states, and success states.
5. Subscription/paywall design should feel premium and justified by the value of the app.
6. The UX should feel intentional and highly polished throughout.

Monetization expectations:
1. Include a thoughtful subscription model.
2. The paid offering must be tied to meaningful recurring value.
3. Make the monetization strategy feel credible and strong enough for a real App Store launch.
4. Include reasoning in docs for why people would pay and what drives retention.

Deliverables:
1. Full Xcode project.
2. Production-quality source code.
3. Detailed README.
4. Product brief.
5. Monetization strategy.
6. Technical architecture notes.
7. QA checklist.
8. App Store readiness notes.
9. Any setup/run instructions needed.
10. A strong Claude Design prompt for future visual refinement.

Important:
- Do not give me just a concept, spec, or prototype.
- Do not stop at planning.
- Actually build the app.
- Make strong product decisions autonomously.
- Favor quality over novelty for novelty’s sake.
- If using AI/LLM/ML/AR, only use it if it creates real value. Do not force it in.
- The final result should feel like a serious startup-quality iOS product someone could refine and launch.

Before building:
Briefly choose the app idea you believe has the strongest combination of:
- monetization potential
- usefulness
- uniqueness
- design potential
- retention
- launch viability

Then build it fully.
```

## Phase Purpose

Decide UX flows, information architecture, key screens/states, and the design principles that govern them.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

I’d keep the app to a very tight eight-screen shape, because this product wins on clarity and trust, not feature count. The real screens are: a launch lock screen that handles Face ID/passcode and the unlocked-with-warning fallback; a short onboarding flow that sells the promise and privacy posture without asking for Contacts; the dashboard; add person; person detail; a dedicated log interaction sheet; the weekly ritual session; and settings, which includes subscription status, export, archived people, and destructive data actions. The paywall should be a destination presented from the dashboard or ritual only when the free top-3 preview is genuinely exceeded, not a floating global interruption.

The primary user flow should be brutally simple. First launch goes through onboarding, offers privacy lock, then lands on an empty dashboard with one dominant action: add someone. The user adds a person, logs a first note immediately, returns to a dashboard that now feels alive even if nobody is due yet, and later comes back into a separate ritual flow when someone crosses the 1.0 warmth ratio. From there the loop is act on a bounded queue, finish the session, see a real completion state, and leave. The important structural rule is that dashboard and ritual cannot collapse into one screen. The dashboard is orientation and reassurance. The ritual is action and relief.

The dashboard should have four distinct states, not one flexible list. Zero people means a high-confidence empty state with one CTA and a short explanation of how the warmth system works. Some people exist but nobody is due means a “you’re in good shape” success state with maybe one subtle secondary action to add a note or review archived people, not a blank screen. A due queue state means ranked cards with the “why now” sentence doing real work. A truncated free-tier state means the same queue, but after the visible top 3 there is a premium break that says exactly how many more are waiting. Exactly 3 due people should show no teaser. Exactly 4 should show 3 plus “1 more waiting.” If that boundary is fuzzy, the paywall will feel cheap.

Add person should be one screen, not a wizard. Name first, cadence defaulted to Regular, save. The first note should be part of the same flow or an immediate follow-on sheet so the app captures value before the user drops out. The optional “key fact” and “open loop” fields belong behind a disclosure row that keeps the fast path fast. The states here are straightforward but important: clean empty form, inline duplicate-name warning that does not block save, validation error for blank name or blank required note, and a clear success handoff back into the dashboard or detail screen. This is the conversion point of the whole product, so extra form chrome is the enemy.

Person detail should lead with meaning, not metadata. The top of the screen should show the person’s name, warmth state, and one plain-language explanation sentence that references the actual last note or lack of one. Below that, a simple reverse-chronological timeline of interactions. The bottom or overflow area should hold lifecycle controls: log outreach, snooze, archive, delete permanently. The states are important here too: no notes yet, active history, archived history, delete confirmation, and re-auth interruption handling where a canceled auth leaves everything untouched. I would not put “edit person” complexity front and center in v1; the relationship record matters more than profile management.

The ritual should feel like a session, not a list. It should open into a bounded queue with visible progress, one person at a time or one primary card at a time, and exactly three actions: log outreach, snooze, archive. The product promise here is psychological: the user should feel that the app reduced background relationship anxiety into a small doable set. So the states need to be designed as deliberately as the happy path: no due people, due queue under the free cap, due queue truncated by the paywall, queue overflow with “N more waiting,” interrupted session after relaunch, and a completion state that names what happened in that session. “You logged 2 follow-ups, snoozed 1, archived 1” is better than generic celebration copy. Snooze should open a real date picker with guardrails against past dates and absurd far-future mute behavior.

Settings should carry the trust burden. It needs to show privacy lock status, subscription status with restore purchases, export, archived people, and full reset/delete options. Export should not feel like a utility hidden in a footer; it is part of the ownership story. The export path needs its own warning state before file generation, and a canceled auth must leave no file behind. Archived people can live here rather than in the main navigation because they are a management edge, not the core loop.

Visually, I would keep the app calm, bright, and expensive-looking rather than dark and “tech.” Warm neutrals, restrained color, generous spacing, and a single accent color used sparingly to signal state changes. The dashboard should feel like a premium briefing surface: large title, calm background, clear card hierarchy, no dense table view energy. Warmth states should not scream in red by default; “cooling” should feel like a nudge, not an alarm. The ritual can take slightly stronger visual framing than the dashboard so it feels like stepping into a focused mode. I’d avoid decorative illustrations unless they genuinely improve empty and completion states. The premium feel should come from pacing, typography, card composition, and copy discipline.

Accessibility needs to be treated as part of the screen contract, not cleanup. The “why now” line on each dashboard or ritual card should be one combined accessibility element so VoiceOver reads it as a coherent sentence. Ritual progress needs an explicit accessible value like “3 of 7.” Completion needs one announced summary instead of scattered labels. The lock and unlocked-warning states need to remain legible at very large Dynamic Type sizes without truncating the privacy explanation or hiding the primary action. Destructive actions must be unmistakable by label and trait, not color alone. If the design only works at default text size and full visual attention, it is not production-ready.

The biggest risk in this direction is that even a polished version can still become tasteful guilt software if the dashboard overemphasizes neglect and the ritual feels like debt service. The mitigation is not just softer copy. It is structural: cap the queue, preserve a real “in good shape” state, keep the explanation specific and humane, let snooze and archive be legitimate exits, and make completion feel like progress rather than temporary reprieve.

The detail I most want the final output to preserve is this: the app’s information architecture must revolve around two different questions. The dashboard answers “who needs me, and why now?” The ritual answers “what is the smallest useful session I can complete this week?” If those collapse into one generalized people list, the product loses its wedge and turns into a prettier database.

**Claude (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

I want to start from the shape of the app rather than a screen list, because if the shape is wrong the screen list won't save it. This app has exactly one home: the Dashboard. Everything else — Add Person, Person Detail, the Ritual, Settings — is either a sheet you complete and dismiss, or a page you push into and back out of. I'd resist a tab bar entirely. A tab bar implies several destinations of equal weight, and this product only has one destination that matters day to day. Ritual is not a tab, it's an event — it should surface as a prominent, dismissible card at the top of the Dashboard when there's something to review ("7 people could use you this week — start the ritual"), and disappear entirely when the queue is empty, replaced by the "you're in good shape" success state. Settings lives behind a single toolbar icon, because it's a place you visit rarely, not a place you live.

Here's the screen inventory as I see it, in the order a real user would hit them:

Launch/Lock screen — the very first thing on every cold launch (and after the ~2-minute background grace period expires). States: authenticating (a calm branded moment, not a spinner staring at you), unlocked-with-warning banner for devices with no biometrics/passcode enrolled, and failure/retry if Face ID fails but a passcode fallback is still available. This screen needs to feel reassuring, not like a bank vault — one clean icon, one line of copy, done.

Onboarding — three short beats, not a marketing carousel. Beat one: what this app is and isn't ("Nurture helps you remember people and notice who's drifting — it never reads your Contacts, messages, or calendar"). Beat two: Face ID/passcode setup, framed as protecting what you write, not as friction. Beat three: straight into "add your first person." No permission prompts, no account creation, no paywall preview yet — the fastest possible route to the first add-person screen, because the whole retention thesis depends on that first note landing inside sixty seconds. If a device has no biometrics/passcode, onboarding still completes and hands off to the persistent warning state rather than stalling.

Add Person (sheet, not full push) — one text field for name, cadence tier as a three-segment picker defaulting to Regular, then straight into a first note. The note field is one line, one tap, always focused when the sheet opens. Underneath it, collapsed, is a "+ add a detail" disclosure that reveals key fact and open loop — closed by default, and the sheet must be fully saveable with that disclosure never touched. A duplicate-name warning appears inline above the save button if there's an exact match — never a blocking alert, just a soft "you already have a Sarah Chen — add another?" that doesn't stop the save.

Dashboard (root) — this is the screen that has to do the most emotional work, so its states matter more than any other screen's. Zero-people empty state: an illustration-and-copy moment that pushes straight to Add Person, framed as "start building your list," not "your list is empty" (empty implies failure, start implies beginning). Populated-but-nobody-due state: the "you're in good shape" success state — this is not a throwaway, it's one of the most important screens in the app because it's the proof the product isn't a guilt machine; it should feel like a genuine reward, not a blank afterthought. Active-queue state: a ranked list of cards, each showing name, a one-line "why now" sentence built from the actual last note, and a subtle color/weight signal for cooling vs cold — never a scary red badge, more like a warm-to-cool gradient so the visual language matches the copy's tone. And the ritual entry card sits pinned above all of this when the queue is non-empty.

Person Detail — pushed from a dashboard card or from anywhere a name appears. Chronological note timeline (most recent first), the live warmth explanation at the top framed as a full sentence a human would say ("It's been 6 weeks since you talked — you usually check in every 2"), and a clear but not alarming path to archive or permanent delete. Delete has to feel meaningfully harder to reach than archive — I'd put archive as a normal toolbar action and bury delete one level further (inside an "manage" menu or the bottom of the screen), styled destructively, with the re-auth challenge appearing before anything is confirmed.

Ritual — a distinct full-screen modal flow, entered deliberately, not something you fall into. One person at a time, front and center, with the same why-now sentence as the dashboard so it doesn't feel like a different app, and three unmistakable actions (Log outreach / Snooze / Archive) as equally-weighted buttons — not a swipe gesture hidden behind discoverability problems, since this is the one flow where every user needs to find all three options without hunting. A visible progress indicator ("3 of 7"). If the queue exceeds the free preview, the paywall interrupts only at the boundary where the free items run out, framed as continuing the session rather than a cold ambush. Completion screen names what happened — "You reached out to 2 people, snoozed 1, and archived 1" — with an exit back to the Dashboard, which should now show the good-shape state if the queue cleared.

Paywall — reached only from the ritual overflow point (never from browsing, never from settings as an unprompted upsell). Needs its own loading (fetching product info), error (StoreKit unreachable — should still let the user close and use the free app fully), and success/restore states.

Settings — subscription status with a working Restore Purchases row, export (with the unencrypted-file warning surfaced before the auth prompt, not after), a link to archived people (they need somewhere to live and be reversible from), and the DEBUG-only panels for anyone building this.

The biggest risk I see in my own direction: if the Dashboard cards lean too hard into "why now" specificity, they risk feeling surveillance-y or over-clever ("you mentioned her promotion 6 weeks ago") when actually the note was one throwaway line — the copy generation has to gracefully degrade to something plain when the last note is thin, or it'll feel like the app is performing intelligence it doesn't have. The detail the final output must preserve: Dashboard and Ritual are visually related (same card language, same why-now sentence) but structurally separate screens with separate entry/exit points — and the good-shape success state on the Dashboard is not a minor edge case, it's core craft that has to get real design attention, not a placeholder "you're all caught up!" label with no thought behind it.

**Gemini (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

From a client-architecture and state-management perspective, the cleanest path is to build Nurture around a single-active-navigation stack backed by a main-actor-isolated repository, where all warmth states and attention listings are dynamically computed at read time rather than persisted as fields. Caching warmth states in SwiftData would create cache-invalidation bugs when shifting the clock in the debug time-travel panel or crossing timezone boundaries, so these must remain computed properties on the model level. To prevent the app from becoming a source of user anxiety, the dashboard needs to act as a structured queue of only those contacts whose computed warmth ratio is equal to or greater than one point zero, sorted descending, with a hard visual cap of ten contacts. For the free tier, we slice this list to three items and render a blurred paywall card only when a fourth due contact is present, ensuring we never show a pricing prompt when the user's queue is naturally small. The weekly ritual session is decoupled from the main dashboard context so that mid-session exits or crashes preserve the user's completed actions without resetting the review queue. 

The biggest risk to this architecture is state desynchronization and sluggishness on background-to-foreground transitions. If Face ID is too aggressive, it will interrupt quick note logging when a user jumps to another app to copy a detail, but if it is too lax, it compromises privacy. A two-minute background grace period handles this balance, but it requires binding scene-phase changes directly to a security coordinator that clears the unlock state and presents a full-screen shield. If biometrics are missing or disabled on the device, the app must fall back to a persistent, dismissible warning on the settings screen rather than throwing unhandled errors or locking the user out.

The final phase output must preserve the exact mathematical rules for warmth transitions, where a contact is warm below one point zero, cooling between one point zero and one point five, and cold at one point five and above, with the boundaries inclusive on the lower side. Additionally, the re-authentication prompt must be explicitly wired to fire at the exact moment of destructive deletion or data export, rather than relying on the general session unlock state. This guarantees that exported files or wipes require an active, fresh biometric check, reinforcing the security posture of the sandbox. I have documented this full layout, including screen inventories, visual systems, and accessibility parameters, in the project specifications file.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Reading through round one, all three actually landed in the same place without much friction — Codex mapped the product flow, Claude fleshed out every screen's craft and states, and Gemini backed both of them up from the architecture side (computed-at-read warmth, main-actor repository, reauth wired to the exact moment of delete/export). Nobody is fighting here: Claude's "no tab bar, ritual as an event not a destination" call lines up cleanly with Gemini's "single-active-navigation stack," and Codex's screen list matches Claude's almost one-for-one, just described from a different angle. The one stylistic difference — Codex sketches a separate "log interaction" sheet while Claude folds the first note into the Add Person flow with an optional follow-on sheet — isn't a real disagreement, just two ways of describing the same "capture value immediately" requirement, and both agree the mechanism (required note, optional disclosure fields, save-without-touching-them) is identical.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screen inventory (8 screens/flows):**
1. **Launch/Lock** — Face ID/passcode gate on cold launch and after ~2min background grace period; states: authenticating, unlocked-with-warning (no biometrics/passcode enrolled), failed-with-passcode-retry.
2. **Onboarding** — three short beats: what the app is/isn't (explicitly: never reads Contacts/messages/calendar), Face ID/passcode setup framed as protection not friction, straight into Add Person. No permission prompts, no paywall preview here.
3. **Add Person** (sheet) — name, cadence segmented picker (default Regular), immediately followed by a required one-line first note; "+ add a detail" disclosure (closed by default) reveals key fact / open loop; inline non-blocking duplicate-name warning.
4. **Dashboard** (the app's one true home, no tab bar) — ranked, explainable attention queue; a ritual-entry card appears pinned above the queue only when something is due, and disappears into the good-shape state when it's not.
5. **Person Detail** — warmth explanation as a full sentence referencing the actual last note, reverse-chronological timeline, archive (easy to reach) and permanent delete (deliberately harder to reach, destructive-styled, fresh re-auth before confirming).
6. **Ritual** (full-screen modal, entered deliberately) — one bounded, capped session; progress indicator; three equally-weighted actions (log outreach / snooze / archive); ends in a completion screen naming the session's specific actions.
7. **Paywall** — a destination reached only from the ritual/dashboard overflow point when the free top-3 is genuinely exceeded, never a floating upsell; own loading/error/success/restore states.
8. **Settings** — privacy lock status, subscription + Restore Purchases, export (warning shown before the auth prompt), archived people, full reset/delete, DEBUG-only panels.

**Primary user flow:** Onboarding (no Contacts ask) → Face ID/passcode setup → empty Dashboard with one dominant CTA → Add Person + first note in the same motion (under a minute) → Dashboard now feels alive even with nobody due yet → once someone crosses warmth ratio 1.0, the ritual-entry card appears → user enters the Ritual, acts on a capped queue, hits a real completion state, returns to Dashboard → paywall only ever appears at the point the free preview is genuinely exceeded.

**State model per screen:** Dashboard has four distinct states (zero-people, populated-but-nobody-due "good shape" success state, active ranked queue, free-tier-truncated with exact 3-no-teaser/4-shows-"1 more waiting" boundary). Add Person has clean-empty, inline duplicate warning (non-blocking), validation error (blank name/note), success handoff. Person Detail has no-notes-yet, active history, archived, delete-confirmation, and canceled-reauth-leaves-everything-untouched. Ritual has no-one-due, under-cap queue, paywall-truncated queue, over-cap "N more waiting," interrupted-and-resumed-after-relaunch, and named completion state. Paywall has loading/error(StoreKit unreachable, app still usable free)/success/restore. All warmth math stays exactly as locked previously: ratio = elapsed/cadence off `max(createdAt,lastInteractionAt)`, warm <1.0, cooling 1.0–<1.5, cold ≥1.5, computed live via `DateProvider`, never cached.

**Visual direction:** Calm, bright, "expensive," not dark/techy — warm neutrals, generous spacing, restrained single accent color reserved for state changes. Warmth signals read as a gradient nudge (warm-to-cool), never alarm-red. Dashboard and Ritual share the same card language and "why now" sentence so they feel like one coherent app, while remaining structurally separate screens with separate entry/exit points. Ritual can take slightly stronger visual framing (a focused "mode") than the calmer dashboard. Illustrations only where they earn their place — the empty and good-shape success states deserve real craft, not placeholder copy.

**Accessibility notes (cross-cutting, per screen, not a separate feature):** each dashboard/ritual card exposes its "why now" sentence as one combined VoiceOver element; ritual progress exposes an explicit accessible value ("3 of 7"); completion screen announces one summary element, not scattered labels; lock/unlocked-warning copy and destructive actions (delete, export) stay legible and unmistakable at accessibility-XXL Dynamic Type, never relying on color alone. Copy for "why now" explanations must gracefully degrade to something plain when the underlying note is thin, so the app never appears to perform intelligence it doesn't have.

**Design principle carried forward as the load-bearing rule for this phase:** Dashboard answers "who needs me, and why now" (orientation/reassurance); Ritual answers "what's the smallest useful session I can finish this week" (bounded action/relief). They share visual language but must never collapse into a single generalized list screen — that's the one failure mode everyone flagged as fatal to the product's wedge.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This clears the bar. All five required outputs are present and concrete: an 8-screen inventory with explicit presentation style (sheet vs. push vs. full-screen modal vs. toolbar-accessed), a primary user flow that traces cold-launch through onboarding, capture, dashboard, ritual, and paywall in one coherent path, a state model naming exact states per screen (including the precise 3-no-teaser/4-teaser boundary and the canceled-reauth-leaves-everything-untouched state), a visual direction with real point of view (warm neutrals, gradient-not-alarm color language, restrained accent), and accessibility notes that match the four concrete checks locked in `app_features` (combined VoiceOver element, "3 of 7" accessible value, one announced completion summary, XXL Dynamic Type legibility on trust/lock screens).

It correctly resolves the one structural question this phase needed to answer — no tab bar, Dashboard as the single home, Ritual as a deliberately-entered full-screen modal rather than a destination — and ties that decision back to the load-bearing design principle from `app_features` (Dashboard = orientation, Ritual = bounded action) without re-litigating it. The apparent Codex/Claude difference (separate log-interaction sheet vs. folded-in first note) is correctly identified as notational, not substantive, since both preserve the same required-note-plus-optional-disclosure mechanism from `next_steps_small`/`app_features`.

No contradictions with locked decisions: ratio math (warm <1.0, cooling 1.0–<1.5, cold ≥1.5), the paywall gating only the ritual queue beyond top-3, DEBUG-only debug panel/Face-ID-disable toggle, export/delete re-auth-at-the-moment requirements, and the no-Contacts-permission onboarding are all carried forward intact. A SwiftUI developer could build the navigation shell and per-screen state machines directly from this output. Good to advance.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Reading through round one, all three actually landed in the same place without much friction — Codex mapped the product flow, Claude fleshed out every screen's craft and states, and Gemini backed both of them up from the architecture side (computed-at-read warmth, main-actor repository, reauth wired to the exact moment of delete/export). Nobody is fighting here: Claude's "no tab bar, ritual as an event not a destination" call lines up cleanly with Gemini's "single-active-navigation stack," and Codex's screen list matches Claude's almost one-for-one, just described from a different angle. The one stylistic difference — Codex sketches a separate "log interaction" sheet while Claude folds the first note into the Add Person flow with an optional follow-on sheet — isn't a real disagreement, just two ways of describing the same "capture value immediately" requirement, and both agree the mechanism (required note, optional disclosure fields, save-without-touching-them) is identical.

CONSENSUS: YES
